text: laparoscopic robotic-assisted Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and hiatal hernia repair. A sixty-six-year-old woman with type II diabetes mellitus, GERD, esophagitis, hiatal hernia, dumping syndrome after cholecystectomy and severe obesity with a BMI of forty kilograms square meters. A gastroscopy was performed for the assessment of the hiatal hernia. H. pylori was negative.

The patient is positioned with open legs, and the assistant stands between them. Trochars are placed for laparoscopic access. Inside the cavity, a large hiatal hernia is visible. Graspers are used to reduce the herniated stomach. The surgery begins with the dissection of the right crus. A latex band is applied for esophageal traction to reduce the abdominal esophagus. A hook instrument is used for mediastinum dissection, offering full robotic articulation. The hiatus is closed with Effibon stitches.

The surgeon dissects the lesser curvature of the stomach and uses a tri-stapler with a 60mm suture to create the gastric pouch. A 36 French bougie is used for calibration. Two 60mm sutures are placed vertically. A 60cm biliopancreatic limb is anastomosed side-to-side with a 150cm alimentary Roux limb using a 60mm tanned cartridge. The gastrojejunum anastomosis is performed in a hand-sewn terminal lateral fashion, with two planes of PDS running sutures.
No drains were used. The patient was discharged at day two after surgery. GERD symptoms resolved.
